body_txt: Sometimes, the only smart people in a work are TV geniuses , absent-minded professors , insufferable geniuses , or representatives of some other variety of " brilliant-automatically-equals-socially-awkward ". One of these may have Einstein Hair or otherwise seriously neglect their appearance. In those works, you can expect them to be more concerned with abstract equations or other intellectual problems than with the real human beings around them. Maybe their intellectualism is just a way of hiding their evil character . Not so with anyone who is a Gentleman and a Scholar. They manage to be both a highly intelligent expert in his chosen field and a pleasant, well-adjusted, and socially engaging human being, sometimes being even more attuned to the nuances of social etiquette than many less-intelligent characters. Frequently, their emphasis is more on the humanities than on the natural sciences (they are probably fluent in dead languages and always handy with an apropos quotation from Aristotle , Saint Augustine , or Dante ), but this is not an absolute rule. Whatever their field, they are always striving to learn more, but never allow themself to fall victim to Crippling Overspecialization . They are always well-groomed and neatly dressed , unless extraordinary circumstances dictate otherwise. Simply put, this is what happens when The Smart Guy is also a Nice Guy . Sometimes, the aforementioned socially awkward intellectuals can fit this. Simply because someone is socially inept does not mean they are rude. Professor Frink and Dr. Brown would never hurt a fly. This character may not necessarily be an actual Blue Blood or Quintessential British Gentleman , but their adherence to the Good Old Ways , their graciousness as a host , and their intelligent and erudite conversations make him seem like one. Unlike the slightly-infantilized geniuses one may see in other works, a Gentleman And A Scholar is always mature and maintains their composure. They are typically older, but if not, expect them to be Wise Beyond His Years . That maturity may or may not make them a Badass Bookworm , but they generally tend to eschew direct physical confrontation if it can be avoided. They frequently stray into Antiquated Linguistics , and possibly the better-done sort of Sesquipedalian Loquaciousness . Their alma mater is probably a school with a long history and Gothic towers . They are more often unwed, but if they have a family, expect them to be a benevolent patriarch . If they go off to war, expect them to receive a commission and become an Officer and a Gentleman (and a Scholar) . This trope is commonly seen in conjunction with Genius Book Club (expect to see many leather-bound books and a bust of Pallas ) and Smart People Play Chess . May or may not smoke a pipe . Glasses are common , but many such characters tend to be older, so perhaps it is justified . Shares a few characteristics with the Gentleman Snarker . Will usually be at least a bit of a Renaissance Man , though what makes them a gentleman and a scholar is not their diversification into multiple fields per se , but rather their ability to maintain his dignity and be socially well-adjusted even while intensively studying an academic subject. Note that this is not always a man , but neither is it strictly a unisex trope . It is possible for a woman to be a Lady and a Scholar, but because women were not common in academia until the 20-21st century, the archetype has not had proper time to develop an easily recognizable Distaff Counterpart . There's also less expectation of Insufferable Genius associated with female academics , so their being warm and well-mannered is less likely to be a defining trait. Historically, this trope is a product of the Enlightenment and the Scientific Revolution. When early modern scientific traditions formed, they were chiefly a province of the gentlemanly class of independently wealthy and landowning men. These were, by and large, the only people who had the money, resources, and leisure time with which to pursue a life of study, experimentation, and exploration for their own sakes. This often led to a scientist's social status and respectability playing a major role in determining the reception of his discoveries and lectures. The modern view of scientists as eccentric, lab-bound recluses only really came into its own with the development of modern academia and government- or company-funded research laboratories in the post-World Wars period. Also see Good Is Not Dumb . Compare and contrast with Kindhearted Simpleton . Polar Opposite Trope of Insufferable Imbecile . If they have magic, they're a Gentleman Wizard . An existing character can be upgraded inexplicably in this way via Instant Cultured .
